title: Lesson 443
transcript: declaring a disaster? This is actually a really interesting question to contemplate. How and when is an organization gonna decide to declare a disaster? So earlier here in domain seven, we talked about incident response and the whole point of having an incident response process is that you can detect if an incident has occurred and then you can do something about it. And so, honestly, the primary way where most bad things are first detected is through your incident response process. But here's where you're gonna have to make a decision. If something bad happens, and it's detected by your instant response process, you're gonna have to make a decision as to whether or not, okay, so this bad thing has just occurred. Are we just gonna activate our instant response and follow our instant response process? Or are you going to say, you know what? Nope. You know, this bad thing that's just happened. We're not going to treat it as an incident. And instead, we're going to declare a disaster and activate our BCP, our DRP plans. So in other words, an organization will have to make a decision. When something bad happens and they detect it through their incident response process, are they just going to continue following their incident response process? Or instead, are they going to declare a disaster and activate their BCP and DRP plans? So the question then becomes is, what's going to drive them to make this decision one way or the other? And I'll give you a giant hint. What's going to be the major driver behind them making the decision is one of the key measurements of time we just talked about. Which measurement of time do you think it is? This one, MTD, Maximum Tolerable Downtime. Or remember, it's also called MAD, Maximum Allowable Downtime. So this is the key measurement of time that we used by an organization to decide, are we gonna treat this as an incident or are we gonna declare a disaster? So here's how this typically works. So something bad happens and it's detected. And you may recall when we talked about instant response, the first thing that an organization is gonna do when something bad happens is they're gonna activate their instant response team, and that instant response team is gonna do an impact assessment. In other words, they're gonna figure out how big of a deal is this bad thing? And how long is it gonna take to recover? So let's say that something bad happens, the IR instant response team is activated. They perform a quick impact assessment and they determine, you know what? If we follow the incident response process, it's going to take us seven days to get things back to business as usual. So. Bad thing happens, instant response team is activated, they do an impact assessment and they determine it's gonna take seven days. If we follow our instant response process, it's gonna take us seven days to get back to business as usual. If the MTD for say that system is only 24 hours, Should you follow the instant response process? No, right, because you're gonna exceed your MTD. Remember, what does MTD mean? Maximum allowable, maximum tolerable downtime? It's the maximum amount of time that something can be down for before, you're no longer a business, right? Before the business is no longer going concern. And so this is why the MTD is the perfect number to use here. So if you determine following the incident response process is gonna take seven days to get back to business as usual, and your MTD is only 24 hours, then you cannot follow your incident response process, right? It's gonna take way too long. You're gonna be out of business before you can get things back up and running. So this is when you would say, nope, right? Because the MTD has been exceeded. We're not going to treat this as an incident. Instead, we're going to declare a disaster. And then you'll activate your relevant BCP and DRP plans. And the whole point of those plans is to make sure that certain critical business processes, certain critical systems are back up and running within the MTD. within say 24 hours in the example I just used there. So that's one of the major criteria that's used as to whether or not to declare a disaster. Like, are we going to treat this as an incident or are we going to declare a disaster? This is a really good thing to remember. If the MTD is going to be exceeded, you're going to declare a disaster. So this is one of the criteria that would be used as part of the declaration criteria. There might be other things that would factor in as well, but remember MTD. If you do decide to declare a disaster, there's lots of communication that's going to have to happen. You're going to need to have a really well-trained team to be able to respond to this. In a disaster, the emergency management team is typically made up of all sorts of stakeholders from all across the organization. Because again, if you're responding to a disaster, it needs to be all hands on deck. Everyone needs to be helping out because otherwise you may not be a business anymore. So your emergency management team is going to be typically made up of senior executives from across the organization, all sorts of relevant stakeholders from human resources, IT, Maybe even public relations. Why public relations? Because maybe CNN is on line three. Maybe your customers are screaming because they're unhappy. So PR may need to be involved. Legal is an interesting one here. Why is legal involved? Well, let me ask you this question. If an organization is, say, storing sensitive data on European Union citizens, so the organization needs to be GDPR compliant, when the organization is in the midst of responding to a disaster, do they get to just ignore their legal and contractual requirements? You know, if they have to be storing the data in a GDPR compliant way, do they get to just ignore that when they're responding to a disaster? No, absolutely not. So this is why legal might even be involved as well. So that when the organization is responding to whatever cataclysmic event is happening. the organization can make sure they're doing it in such a way that they remain in compliance with whatever their contractual or regulatory requirements are. So this is why emergency management teams are often made up by a bunch of different stakeholders. As I mentioned, there's all sorts of communications that are going to be happening internal to various business units, system owners, senior management, etc., and all sorts of communications externally to customers, investors, regulators, the media, etc., So there you go. That's some of the things to be thinking about as a part of declaring and responding to a disaster.
